title: Best Picture Power Rankings: An 'Avatar: The Way of Water' Surge and a Surprise No. 1
format: Sean, Amanda and Joanna Robinson talk 'Women Talking,' then re-rank the Best Picture race as 'Avatar: The Way of Water' surges and the streaming bump lands 'Banshees' at a shaky No. 1. Plus: Amanda's chat with Sarah Polley.

films.title: Women Talking
films.note: Sarah Polley's adaptation of Miriam Toews's 2018 novel, out in limited release. Eight women from an isolated Mennonite colony debate whether to stay or leave after a series of assaults; the seeming period piece is actually set in 2010. Joanna was riveted; Sean was moved, especially by Claire Foy. Amanda hit the 'it's a play' objection and never fully got past the talkiness or the drained, desaturated Luc Montpellier cinematography. They flag the invented centrality of Ben Whishaw's August as the film's one real flaw, and Rooney Mara as the deliberate light-of-hope.

segments.heading: Best Picture Power Rankings
segments.note: best to worst - a live, admittedly toxic re-rank
segments.subject: the 2023 Best Picture race

films.title: The Banshees of Inisherin
films.note: No. 1 on the strength of the HBO Max streaming bump, which all three call a live-needle read rather than a real prediction. Nobody thinks it wins, but the Farrell/Gleeson/Condon/Keoghan acting slate keeps it near the top.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: The least divisive film with proven sticking power, riding what Sean calls the best campaign of the year from A24. Only drag: a fan/reply-guy problem that could breed discourse fatigue.

films.title: TÁR
films.note: The high-minded pick the literate branch wants; Scorsese praised it at the New York Film Critics Circle awards. Sean gives it a 0 percent chance to win but happily plants it at three.

films.title: Elvis
films.note: The surge nobody on the podcast can explain, powered by an Austin Butler youth wave. Great fat-Elvis ending buried under long stretches of musician-biopic filler; Amanda refuses to accept it belongs this high.

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: The surge that reshaped the list. A box-office monster gaining daily, up from No. 10 last time; now nobody thinks it misses. Amanda has a physical reaction watching it climb over Top Gun.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Still a lock to be nominated and a consensus crowd-pleaser, but the Avatar wave knocks it a slot as the two blockbusters jockey.

films.title: The Fabelmans
films.note: A precipitous fall from post-Toronto front-runner to seven, because almost nobody saw it and it never made a pop-culture dent. It's on VOD but not yet on a streaming service, which the group thinks matters.

films.title: Triangle of Sadness
films.note: Joanna's undervalued pick from last time; she's now confident it lands a nomination, especially with international voters. 'The French love Triangle of Sadness.'

films.title: Babylon
films.note: Sean's heart pick, placed at nine after he lobbies for it at No. 1. Two hours of great movie inside a knockout; Amanda and Joanna are cooler, but they see the Academy barely squeaking it in - the year's Nightmare Alley.

films.title: Women Talking
films.note: Barely on the list at 10, a film they think the Academy wants to nominate but keeps kicking down the release calendar. Vulnerable in part because TÁR already occupies its high-minded lane; Frances McDormand's producing muscle is the counter-argument.

segments.heading: Sarah Polley
segments.note: Amanda's chat with the 'Women Talking' writer-director

films.title: Women Talking
films.note: Polley on rewriting the rules of the film set with Dee Dee Gardner and Frances McDormand - 10-hour days, everyone home for bedtime - after a three-and-a-half-year concussion made her doubt she could direct again. Miriam Toews's guiding note: don't lose the laughter. She kept the film under 100 minutes so it 'moves like a bullet.'

films.title: Stories We Tell
films.note: Her last feature before the long gap; the reinvention of documentary form Sean was excited about. After it she raised kids and spent a decade writing - a book, a miniseries, scripts - which made her a far more rigorous, many-drafts screenwriter.

films.title: Brother
films.note: Polley's last-great-thing pick, Clement Virgo's Canadian film she calls a favorite of the last few years, with performances by Aaron Pierre and Lamar Johnson; she saw it at the Toronto Film Festival to a screaming, weeping standing ovation.

referenced.group: In the running, cut from the 10
